learn.title: Flow Batteries for Grid Storage
learn.intro: Flow batteries store energy using liquid electrolytes, enabling scalable grid-scale storage. Vanadium and iron systems offer long-duration solutions critical for balancing renewable energy intermittency and ensuring reliable power supply 24/7.
learn.c1_text: Vanadium batteries use vanadium ions in liquid solutions stored in tanks. Unlike lithium-ion, they scale energy capacity by increasing tank size. A 100 MW/400 MWh system can power 40,000 homes for 4 hours, ideal for grid stabilization.
learn.c2_text: Iron-air systems react iron with atmospheric oxygen, offering ultra-low-cost storage. Form Energy's 100-hour iron-air battery uses scrap iron, promising $20/kWh costs by 2030. Their 300 MWh project in Massachusetts demonstrates scalable long-duration storage potential.
learn.c3_text: India's Ministry of New & Renewable Energy tests vanadium flow systems in Gujarat and Tamil Nadu. The 10 MW/40 MWh project supports solar microgrids, aligning with India's 500 GWh grid storage target by 2030 under the National Energy Storage Mission.
learn.stat1: Vanadium flow batteries achieve 95% round-trip efficiency (2023)
learn.stat2: Iron-air batteries project $20/kWh cost by 2030 (Form Energy)
learn.stat3: India's grid storage target: 500 GWh by 2030 (MNRE)
learn.stat4: 100 MW/400 MWh vanadium system powers 40,000 homes for 4 hours

learn.title: Power Purchase Agreements Explained
learn.intro: Power Purchase Agreements (PPAs) unlock financing for renewable projects by guaranteeing long-term energy sales. They enable developers to secure upfront capital while offering buyers stable, low-cost clean energy, accelerating the global shift to renewables.
learn.c1_text: A PPA is a 15-25 year contract where a buyer (like a utility or corporation) purchases electricity from a project at a fixed rate. For example, a 20-year solar PPA at $30/MWh locks in prices, shielding buyers from market volatility while funding project construction.
learn.c2_text: PPAs provide revenue certainty, allowing developers to secure bank loans or bonds. A 100 MW solar project might raise $150 million via a PPA-backed loan, with repayments tied to guaranteed energy sales. This de-risks investment, reducing financing costs by 2-3% compared to unsubsidized projects.
learn.c3_text: Indian companies like Tata Steel use PPAs to procure 500 MW of renewable energy at 2.5% below grid tariffs. The Ministry of New & Renewable Energy's Green Energy Corral initiative aims to facilitate 50 GW of corporate PPAs by 2030, boosting domestic renewable deployment.
learn.stat1: Global PPA deals reached $12 billion in 2023 (BloombergNEF)
learn.stat2: Corporate PPAs in India: 12.5 GW signed in 2023
learn.stat3: Average PPA price: $28/MWh for utility-scale solar (2023)
learn.stat4: Corporate PPAs reduce financing costs by 2-3% vs unsubsidized projects

learn.title: Green Bonds for Climate Finance
learn.intro: Green bonds mobilize private capital for renewable energy by linking debt instruments to environmental projects. They provide transparent funding pathways for solar, wind, and storage initiatives, driving the $4 trillion annual investment needed for net-zero transitions globally.
learn.c1_text: Green bonds are debt securities earmarked for climate-positive projects. Proceeds fund renewable energy, energy efficiency, or clean transport. For instance, Apple's $2.2 billion green bond in 2023 financed solar farms and battery storage, with third-party verification ensuring environmental integrity.
learn.c2_text: Global green bond issuance hit $500 billion in 2023, with 30% allocated to renewable energy. The European Investment Bank's €750 million green bond for offshore wind demonstrates how these instruments de-risk large-scale projects, offering investors ESG-aligned returns while accelerating clean energy deployment.
learn.c3_text: India's National Hydrogen Mission issued $1.5 billion green bonds for renewable hydrogen production. The Power Grid Corporation's $500 million green bond funds transmission infrastructure, supporting 100 GW renewable capacity integration. The Reserve Bank of India mandates 25% green allocation for institutional investors by 2025.
learn.stat1: Global green bond issuance: $500 billion (2023, Climate Bonds Initiative)
learn.stat2: 30% of green bonds fund renewable energy projects
learn.stat3: India's green bonds: $3.5 billion raised for clean energy (2023)
learn.stat4: EIB's €750 million offshore wind green bond (2023)

learn.title: Renewable Purchase Obligation in India
learn.intro: India's Renewable Purchase Obligation (RPO) mandates distribution companies (DISCOMs) to source a percentage of power from renewables. This policy drives domestic renewable capacity growth, addressing energy security and climate commitments under the nation's 500 GW non-fossil target by 2030.
learn.c1_text: RPO requires DISCOMs to procure 25.23% renewable energy by 2025-26, escalating to 43.32% by 2030-31. Non-compliance incurs penalties of ₹2,500 per MW shortfall annually. For example, Tamil Nadu's DISCOMs must source 14.5% wind/solar in 2024, ensuring steady demand for renewable projects.
learn.c2_text: DISCOMs face financial stress, with 80% defaulting on renewable power payments in 2022. To address this, the government launched ₹1.5 trillion subsidies and the Green Energy Corral platform to facilitate corporate PPAs. Solar parks like Bhadla (2.25 GW) help DISCOMs meet targets efficiently.
learn.c3_text: States like Gujarat and Rajasthan exceed RPO targets via solar parks and wind farms. Karnataka's 2023 compliance at 28% vs 24.5% target highlights success. The MNRE's 2023 guidelines now allow 10% of RPO to be met through renewable energy certificates (RECs), easing DISCOM compliance pressures.
learn.stat2: DISCOMs defaulted on ₹1.2 trillion renewable payments in 2022
learn.stat3: Tamil Nadu's DISCOMs require 14.5% renewable procurement in 2024
learn.stat4: ₹1.5 trillion subsidy allocated for RPO compliance (2023-24)

learn.title: Floating Offshore Wind Platforms
learn.intro: Floating offshore wind platforms unlock energy potential in deep waters beyond fixed-bottom limitations. These innovative structures enable access to stronger, more consistent winds, accelerating renewable energy deployment in regions like the U.S. West Coast and Europe's Atlantic margins.
learn.c1_text: Floating platforms use mooring systems and cables to connect turbines to shore. The Hywind Scotland project uses spar-buoy designs with turbines anchored 115m below sea level. Each 6 MW turbine generates enough power for 13,000 homes, operating efficiently in waters up to 500m deep where fixed foundations are unfeasible.
learn.c2_text: Floating wind reduces levelized cost of energy (LCOE) by 40% by 2030 as technology matures. The U.S. Department of Energy's 2023 cost targets $90/MWh by 2030. These platforms also minimize seabed disruption and support marine biodiversity through artificial reef effects.
learn.c3_text: India's National Offshore Wind Energy Policy targets 60 GW by 2030, with 30% from floating systems. Kerala's Kollam district plans a 1 GW floating wind project in 300m depth waters, leveraging the country's 7,500 km coastline. The Ministry of New & Renewable Energy is collaborating with Norway on technology transfer.
learn.stat1: Hywind Scotland: World's first commercial floating wind farm (2017)
learn.stat2: Floating wind LCOE target: $90/MWh by 2030 (US DOE)
learn.stat3: India's offshore wind target: 60 GW by 2030 (MNRE)
learn.stat4: Floating turbines operate efficiently in waters up to 500m deep

learn.title: Hydrogen Color Code Explained
learn.intro: The hydrogen color code identifies production methods and environmental impact. Green hydrogen from renewables, blue from fossil fuels with carbon capture, and grey from unabated fossil fuels. Understanding these distinctions is vital for decarbonizing sectors like steel, chemicals, and heavy transport globally.
learn.c1_text: Green hydrogen uses renewable electricity to split water (electrolysis), producing zero emissions. Blue hydrogen starts with natural gas but captures 90% of CO2 via carbon capture and storage (CCS). For example, a 100 MW electrolyzer produces 1,000 kg/hour green H2, while blue H2 from gas reforming costs $1.50-2.50/kg vs $3-4/kg for green.
learn.c2_text: Grey hydrogen is made via steam methane reforming without CCS, emitting 10 tons of CO2 per ton of H2. It dominates today's market (95% globally) at $1-1.50/kg but contributes to 830 million tons of annual CO2. The IEA estimates grey H2 will decline to 50% by 2030 as green/blue alternatives scale.
learn.c3_text: India's National Green Hydrogen Mission targets 5 MT/year green hydrogen production by 2030 with $2.5 billion subsidies. Projects like Adani's 8 GW solar-powered electrolyzer in Gujarat aim for 1.5 MT/year green H2. The government mandates 10% blending in city natural gas by 2025, prioritizing green over grey variants.
learn.stat2: Blue H2 captures 90% of CO2 vs grey's 0% (IEA)
learn.stat3: India's green H2 target: 5 million tonnes/year by 2030 (MNRE)
learn.stat4: Grey H2 accounts for 95% of global production (IEA, 2023)
